titleStmt.title: Letter from Richard Perceval-Maxwell to Colonel Robert Perceval-Maxwell, 6 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Richard Henry Perceval-Maxwell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Lieutenant Richard Henry Perceval-Maxwell (1897-1918), to his father, Colonel Robert David Perceval Maxwell, (1870-1932) updating his father on his whereabouts. Richard is unsure of where he will be statoned enxt but prmises to inform his father as soon as he knows for himself.Lieutenant Richard Henry Perceval-Maxwell served in the Cameronians (Scottish Rifles) during the Great War, along with his father and brother. Richard was killed in action in July 1918. Colonel Robert Maxwell was wounded twice while serving with the 36th (Ulster) Division during the First World War..

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Perceval-Maxwell Papers D/1556/27/3/2/2, Perceval-Maxwell Papers D/1556/27/3/2/2

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
20th I.B.D. A.P.O. S. 17 B. E. F. France May 6th Dear Father I'm afraid its
a long time since I wrote
you a letter. You'll see
That I'm out again now.
I came across from Folkestone
on Friday, stayed the night
in Boulogue & proceeded
here the next day. I had
from 10am till 6 to wait in
Folkestone and just as I
was leaving the station to
stroll a bout I ran slick
into Eileen Farnham &
family so I spent the day
with them. It was awfully
lucky. I hope you are having
a fairly good time where you
are. I haven't the faintest
idea which Battalion I'm going
The 9th & tenth are at Arras so the
1st is there also I think the 2nd I
believe is at St Quentin. This is
only hearsay so I'm not sure.
I'll let you know as
soon as I know myself.
Well I'm must end now
so 'Bonne chance' Yrs aff. Dick
